g.text: PTRACE_ATTACH Lifecycle: Attach → Stop → Options → Mid-Syscall State
── STEP 6: First Event — Two Possible Paths ──

text.tspan: [resume Python — stop at next syscall boundary]
[resume to exit stop of interrupted read]

div.p: pid_map_get(&g_pid_map, 1234) → new pid_state_t slot created .pid = 1234 .active = 1 .in_syscall = 0 ← may be WRONG if mid-syscall .outbuf_len = 0 .entry_time = {0,0} (unset)
in_syscall==1 → ENTRY path extract_args(&regs, &state->current) clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &entry_time) build outbuf: '[pid 1234] read(' outbuf_len > 0 → WAIT for exit stop to complete line

in_syscall==1 → ENTRY path BUT: kernel interrupted read() at syscall ENTRY point before kernel executed it Kernel delivers SIGSTOP → read() never ran Result: we see the EXIT stop first next stop will be EXIT (toggle → 0)
flip in_syscall: 1 → 0 call handle_syscall_stop(1234, state) in_syscall==0 → EXIT path BUT outbuf_len == 0 ← NO entry was buffered!

After mid-syscall exit handled: state->in_syscall = 0 state->outbuf_len = 0 NEXT stop will be a genuine ENTRY stop → toggle: 0 → 1 ✓ (ENTRY path) NEXT+1 stop will be EXIT stop → toggle: 1 → 0 ✓ (EXIT path) Toggle is now synchronized with Python's actual syscall progression. All subsequent pairs decoded correctly.

Mid-syscall first exit stop: entry_time = {0, 0} (never set) elapsed_ns would = time since boot ← INVALID → stats_record() NOT called for this stop → stats_record() resumes normally from next full pair

Time measurement for first complete pair after attach includes: (b) tracer context-switch cost ~1–5µs (c) PTRACE_GETREGS overhead ~1µs (d) context-switch back ~1–5µs (e) ptrace stop overhead ~1µs Fast syscalls (getpid ~100ns real) measure as ~10–50µs under ptrace — 100–500× inflated. Only relative comparisons and call counts are meaningful from -c statistics.
